summary: The q4 dataset contains 10 problems (q1-q10), each currently showing 100 issues with no proof-status differentiation available. Because the provided context lacks per-problem breakdowns, severity tags, or resolution timestamps, no problem can be distinguished as having more traction or being closer to a solution at this time. The uniform issue counts strongly suggest a placeholder, capped, or unparsed data state rather than genuine activity signals.
problems: All 10 problems (q1-q10) report an identical 100 issues, which likely indicates a display cap, default placeholder, or data ingestion error rather than real per-problem activity.
The 'Proof Status' section contains only issue counts and no actual proof state labels (e.g., open, in-progress, verified), making solvability assessment impossible.
No metadata on issue type, severity, age, or assignee is present, so common patterns in open issues cannot be reliably extracted.
No timestamps or trend data are available, preventing identification of which problems have rising or declining momentum.
highlights: A complete and consistent set of 10 problems is being tracked, providing a clear scope for the q4 dataset.
Issue tracking infrastructure is in place and reporting uniformly across all problems, a good foundation once richer data is wired in.

suggested_todos.title: Investigate why every problem reports exactly 100 issues; verify whether a cap or ingestion bug is masking true counts
Enrich the Proof Status feed with actual proof states (open, in-progress, verified) instead of raw issue counts
Add issue metadata (type, severity, age, assignee) to enable pattern detection and solvability scoring
Introduce timestamp/trend tracking to surface which problems are gaining traction week over week
Re-run this insight report once corrected per-problem data is available to identify genuine priorities
